This is general test case best practice advice:  Do *not* leave global state modified outside the scope of a test.  It's ok to mess with global state in a test if there's no better way to test what you need to test, but you *have* to put the state back to the way it initially was.

Here, if the vars you're deleting were defined in os.environ before the test, this setUp() is deleting these values and not restoring them after the test executes.  This can lead to all sorts of hard to track down problems where the test order may affect whether or not a test passes depending on if it passes before/after the global state modification.

That being, said, just use `BaseEnvVar` to handle the patching of env vars for you.

For example, I've written a pluggable converter class that uses this facility to export [JSON API](http://jsonapi.org/) -compliant payloads instead of flat dictionaries.

The default implementation is super simple and could indeed be replaced with a plain function. It mainly exists to serve as a model implementation.

(Note that the hooks were implemented later — have a look at the current revision.)

If the `last_child` has no `end_col_offset` attribute set, then this node has no business having its own `end_col_offset` set.

You can think of what you're doing as building up correct `end_col_offset` from the leaves upwards in an AST. No "holes" are permitted. Of course, this is why I had you start with `Const` and `BinOp`, because those form a fully self-contained recursive structure in which each node can be assured to have `end_col_offset` set.

So it is actually more appropriate for you to have an `assert` statement enforcing this condition, rather than calling `set_end_col_offset_by_string`.
While it is appropriate to create a node for a concrete type, you don't need a separate helper fir it. In particular, don't change any of the other instance variables here.
The classes `Name`, `Tuple`, and `List` are all subclasses of `Node`, so you don't need to write the "Node" explicitly in the type. Doing just `(Name | Tuple | List)` conveys the right information.
